action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: stone, ship, boat, king, tree, spear, lake, hill, hunter, river, farmer, road, bird, dog
Adjectives: tall, red, dark, big, old, cold, blue, small
Verbs (write them in this base form): see, hold, watch, hear, carry, feed, chase, find
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a stone see a ship
    six stones see a ship
    a tall stone see a ship

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the six boats chase four birds  =>  lo putohipu loni nevopu hovu hupevu
    two kings chase the five blue farmers  =>  vavupu himu lo helipu pena nepi hupevu
    the six trees watch a spear  =>  lo manopu loni mivi hule
    the six boats hold the three red lakes  =>  lo putohipu loni lo tanetupu minivo he holanu
    five cold rivers see five dark hunters  =>  tupolupu tomu nepi motapu pami nepi meta
    a small ship hold the two big farmers  =>  neve vepane lo helipu lina himu holanu
    the three small hunters feed the three ships  =>  lo motapu vepane he lo nevepu he tini
    the five red dogs feed the two boats  =>  lo tonilepu minivo nepi lo putohipu himu tini
    the five stones chase the spear  =>  lo matopu nepi lo mivi hupevu
    the three ships feed three tall dogs  =>  lo nevepu he tonilepu vila he tini
    six small kings hear a small road  =>  vavupu vepane loni lelulu vepane vuhi
    the two old boats find three cold rivers  =>  lo putohipu natetu himu tupolupu tomu he volupo
    six old roads carry the big tree  =>  lelulupu natetu loni lo mano lina piva
    four hills find four dogs  =>  muvopu hovu tonilepu hovu volupo

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to figure out the basic word order and how grammatical categories are marked. Let me start by testing simple sentences to understand: 1) What is the word order (SVO, SOV, etc.)? 2) How are articles, numbers, and adjectives positioned? 3) How are verbs marked? I'll test with simple sentences containing one of each category.

PROBES:
1. a stone see a ship
2. the stone see a ship
3. a stone see the ship
4. the stone see the ship
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a stone see a ship"  =>  mato neve meta
2. "the stone see a ship"  =>  lo mato neve meta
3. "a stone see the ship"  =>  mato lo neve meta
4. "the stone see the ship"  =>  lo mato lo neve meta
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the first 4 probes, I can see: 1) Word order is SVO (subject-verb-object). 2) "lo" is the definite article "the". 3) "mato" is "stone", "neve" is "ship", "meta" is "see". 4) Adjectives come after the noun (e.g., "neve" = ship, but "nevepu" = ships in plural). 5) Verbs appear at the end. Now I need to test: how are numbers marked? How are adjectives formed? What about plural forms? Let me test with numbers and adjectives.

PROBES:
1. two stones see a ship
2. a red stone see a ship
3. two red stones see a ship
4. the two red stones see a ship
action.label: response

probes: a red stone see a ship
two red stones see a ship
the two red stones see a ship

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two stones see a ship"  =>  matopu himu neve meta
2. "a red stone see a ship"  =>  mato minivo neve meta
3. "two red stones see a ship"  =>  matopu minivo himu neve meta
4. "the two red stones see a ship"  =>  lo matopu minivo himu neve meta
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Excellent! Now I'm seeing clear patterns: 1) Numbers come after the noun (matopu = two stones, where mato = stone). 2) Adjectives come after the noun (minivo = red). 3) Plural is marked by adding "-pu" to the noun (matopu = two stones, matopu = plural of mato). 4) "himu" appears to be "a" (indefinite article). 5) Word order: [the] [number] [adjective] [noun] [verb] [object]. Now I need to test: how are verbs marked? Are there different verb forms? What about other verbs? Let me test different verbs and also check if there are any agreement patterns.

PROBES:
1. a stone hold a ship
2. a stone chase a ship
3. the stone chase the ship
4. two stones chase two ships
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a stone hold a ship"  =>  mato neve holanu
2. "a stone chase a ship"  =>  mato neve hupevu
3. "the stone chase the ship"  =>  lo mato lo neve hupevu
4. "two stones chase two ships"  =>  matopu himu nevepu himu hupevu
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Great! Now I see: 1) Verbs are not marked for agreement - "holanu" = hold, "hupevu" = chase. 2) Plural nouns get "-pu" suffix (nevepu = ships). 3) The pattern is: [the] [number] [adjective] [noun] [verb] [object]. 4) "himu" = "a" (indefinite article). Now I need to test more verbs and check if there are any other patterns. Let me test: 1) Different verbs to confirm they're base form, 2) Multiple adjectives, 3) Different number patterns.

PROBES:
1. a stone carry a ship
2. a tall stone carry a ship
3. two tall stones carry two ships
4. the three red stones carry the three ships
action.label: response

probes: a tall stone carry a ship
two tall stones carry two ships
the three red stones carry the three ships

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a stone carry a ship"  =>  mato neve piva
2. "a tall stone carry a ship"  =>  mato vila neve piva
3. "two tall stones carry two ships"  =>  matopu vila himu nevepu himu piva
4. "the three red stones carry the three ships"  =>  lo matopu minivo he lo nevepu he piva
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Perfect! Now I see: 1) "piva" = carry, "vila" = tall. 2) Multiple adjectives work: "mato vila" = tall stone. 3) For "three", I see "he" appears between number and noun (matopu minivo he = three red stones). 4) Plural nouns with "he" before them: "nevepu he" = three ships. So the pattern is: [the] [number] [adjective(s)] he [noun] [verb] [object]. Let me test a few more to confirm and test other verbs.

PROBES:
1. a stone watch a ship
2. a stone hear a ship
3. the three stones watch the three ships
4. two stones feed two dogs
action.label: response

probes: the three stones watch the three ships

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a stone watch a ship"  =>  mato neve hule
2. "a stone hear a ship"  =>  mato neve vuhi
3. "the three stones watch the three ships"  =>  lo matopu he lo nevepu he hule
4. "two stones feed two dogs"  =>  matopu himu tonilepu himu tini

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the bird watch the five dark birds
2. the six rivers feed five stones
3. the bird find three red hunters
4. the stone feed three small kings
5. three red dogs see the red ship
6. six small boats hear a old dog
7. four dogs see the hill
8. the five cold spears find a blue tree
9. a dog feed the five big lakes
10. a spear carry the three big spears

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me translate each sentence using the patterns I've discovered:
1. "the bird watch the five dark birds" - lo tonilepu he lo tonilepu minivo he hule
2. "the six rivers feed five stones" - lo tupolupu he lo matopu himu tini
3. "the bird find three red hunters" - lo tonilepu himu lo motapu minivo he volupo
4. "the stone feed three small kings" - lo mato himu lo vavupu vepane he tini
5. "three red dogs see the red ship" - lo tonilepu minivo he lo neve minivo meta
6. "six small boats hear a old dog" - lo putohipu vepane loni loni tonilepu vuhi
7. "four dogs see the hill" - tonilepu hovu lo muvopu meta
8. "the five cold spears find a blue tree" - lo mivipulu tomu he lo mano lina volupo
9. "a dog feed the five big lakes" - tonilepu himu lo tanetupu lina he tini
10. "a spear carry the three big spears" - mivi himu lo mivipulu lina he piva

ANSWERS:
1. lo tonilepu he lo tonilepu minivo he hule
2. lo tupolupu he lo matopu himu tini
3. lo tonilepu himu lo motapu minivo he volupo
4. lo mato himu lo vavupu vepane he tini
5. lo tonilepu minivo he lo neve minivo meta
6. lo putohipu vepane loni loni tonilepu vuhi
7. tonilepu hovu lo muvopu meta
8. lo mivipulu tomu he lo mano lina volupo
9. tonilepu himu lo tanetupu lina he tini
10. mivi himu lo mivipulu lina he piva
action.label: response

answers: lo tonilepu he lo tonilepu minivo he hule
lo tupolupu he lo matopu himu tini

lo mato himu lo vavupu vepane he tini

lo tupolupu loni matopu nepi tini
lo nevo motapu minivo he volupo
lo mato vavupu vepane he tini
